# **Section 7: Product Claim Review**

## **Eyebrow**

PRODUCT CLAIMS

## **Headline**

Claims need review before commerce media moves.

## **Body**

Product media often carries claims.

A product does something.  
A feature has a benefit.  
A comparison is made.  
A material is described.  
A price or offer is shown.  
A performance result appears.  
A creator gives an endorsement.  
A product is positioned against another option.

Those claims need clear review before they become public, paid, marketplace-facing, product-page-facing, or reused.

## **Claim Review Triggers**

Performance claim  
Comparison claim  
Product benefit claim  
Health or beauty claim  
Financial claim  
Technical claim  
Sustainability claim  
Material claim  
Offer or pricing claim  
Before-and-after claim  
Creator endorsement  
Marketplace copy  
Paid media use  
Legal-sensitive wording

## **Outcome**

Teams know when product, legal, brand, or compliance review is required before content moves forward.

---

# **Section 8: Talent, Creator, and NILP® Review**

## **Eyebrow**

TALENT AND NILP®

## **Headline**

Creator-led product media needs usage controls.

## **Body**

Commerce media often uses creators, influencers, models, athletes, ambassadors, employees, artists, voice, likeness, product demos, testimonials, and social reposts.

That creates questions around name, image, likeness, persona, voice, consent, usage scope, channel, territory, duration, paid media, and reuse.

Creation Skills helps place those questions inside the production path.

## **Review Triggers**

Creator content included  
Talent likeness used  
Voice used  
NILP® present  
Product demo includes identifiable person  
Testimonial used  
Paid media planned  
New channel added  
New territory added  
Usage window extended  
Asset reused  
Asset localized  
Asset cut down  
Creator repost requested  
Contract limits apply

## **Outcome**

Creator-led and talent-led commerce media moves with clearer review, approval, and recordkeeping.

---

# **Section 9: Marketplace and Channel Versions**

## **Eyebrow**

MARKETPLACE AND CHANNEL VERSIONS

## **Headline**

Every channel should not reinvent the rules.

## **Body**

Product content often needs to be adapted for different platforms, marketplaces, retailers, paid channels, social formats, and regions.

That adaptation needs rules.

A marketplace may require a format.  
A retailer may need different product language.  
A paid channel may need claim review.  
A market may need localization.  
A social version may use talent differently.  
A product page may need updated facts.

Creation Skills helps define when versioning is allowed, when review is required, and what record should exist.

## **Versioning Questions**

What was the original approved copy?

Who approved it?

Which product facts were approved?

Did the claim change?

Did the channel change?

Did the territory change?

Is paid media included?

Is talent or NILP® present?

Is the asset being localized?

Has the product information changed?

Does marketplace review apply?

What record should be kept?

## **Outcome**

Commerce teams can adapt product media with more confidence and less reliance on informal approval.

---

# **Section 10: Asset Reuse and Refresh**

## **Eyebrow**

ASSET REUSE

## **Headline**

Product media needs a rule set after first use.

## **Body**

Product assets rarely stay in one place.

A product video becomes a social cutdown.  
A PDP image becomes a marketplace asset.  
A creator demo becomes paid media.  
A launch asset becomes a seasonal refresh.  
A product claim gets reused in another channel.  
A comparison copy appears in a new campaign.

Creation Skills helps teams define when reuse is allowed, when review is required, and what needs to be recorded.

## **Reuse Questions**

What was the original approved use?

Who approved it?

What rights were granted?

Was talent or NILP® present?

Was product claim review completed?

What channel was approved?

What territory was approved?

Was paid media included?

Was the usage window limited?

Has the asset changed?

Has the product information changed?

Does the new use require additional review?

What record should be kept?

## **Outcome**

Product media can be reused with stronger controls and less dependence on memory.

---

# **Section 11: Delivery Records**

## **Eyebrow**

DELIVERY RECORDS

## **Headline**

Finished commerce media needs a clear record.

## **Body**

A final product media package should not only include files.

It should include enough context for the company to understand what was approved, what product facts were reviewed, what rights were checked, what version moved forward, and what record exists.

## **Delivery Record May Include**

Asset name  
Asset type  
Product or SKU reference  
Owner  
Approver  
Approval date  
Product claim status  
Rights status  
NILP® status  
Consent status  
Usage scope  
Territory  
Channel  
Format  
Version  
Source material notes  
Marketplace status  
Reuse status  
Escalation notes  
Final delivery status  
Audit reference

## **Outcome**

Delivery becomes easier to govern, reuse, and connect to Creation Rights where needed.

---

SYSTEM REPAIR

# **Hero**

## **Eyebrow**

SPORTS AND LIVE MEDIA TEAMS

## **Headline**

For teams turning live moments into approved media.

## **Subhead**

Creation Skills helps sports media groups, leagues, clubs, event teams, sponsors, and live production teams redesign how highlights, athlete content, sponsor assets, social cutdowns, recaps, and campaign materials move from capture to approval to delivery.

## **Body**

Sports and live media move fast.

A game becomes highlights.  
A highlight becomes a social cutdown.  
An athlete moment becomes a sponsor asset.  
A recap becomes paid media.  
A press clip becomes a partner package.  
A behind-the-scenes video becomes a campaign asset.  
A live event becomes dozens of versions across platforms, markets, partners, and rights holders.

The work moves across content, production, athletes, agents, leagues, clubs, sponsors, broadcasters, legal, brand, social, partnerships, and platform teams.

Creation Skills helps sports and live media teams build the company flow behind that work: the people, production agents, operating files, approval gates, athlete NILP® checks, sponsor review, rights checks, reuse rules, records, and Creation Rights readiness needed before media moves forward.

## **Primary CTA**

Book a C/Skills Review

## **Secondary CTA**

Explore Engagements

---

# **Section 1: The Sports Media Problem**

## **Eyebrow**

THE PROBLEM

## **Headline**

Live media moves faster than approval can follow.

## **Body**

Sports and live media teams are built for speed.

The moment happens.  
The clip is cut.  
The caption is drafted.  
The sponsor asks for a version.  
The athlete team wants approval.  
The broadcaster has rights limits.  
The league has usage rules.  
The social team needs to post.  
The paid media team wants to boost.  
The partner team wants to reuse.  
The record is often created after the work already moved.

That speed is part of the value.

But without a clear company flow, teams can lose control over talent usage, sponsor obligations, rights restrictions, NILP® review, platform versions, and final approval records.

Creation Skills helps fix the operating layer around live media production.

---

# **Section 2: What We Help With**

## **Eyebrow**

WHAT WE HELP WITH

## **Headline**

People, agents, athletes, sponsors, approvals, and rights in one company flow.

## **Body**

Creation Skills works with sports and live media teams to clarify how event media is prepared, reviewed, approved, delivered, reused, and recorded.

## **Support Areas**

### **Sports Media Operating Model**

Define how highlights, recaps, sponsor assets, athlete content, social packages, partner materials, and live-event media move from capture to approval.

### **Company-Owned Production Agents**

Design production agents with defined jobs, owners, approved tasks, blocked actions, operating files, and human checkpoints.

### **Operating Files**

Turn scattered prompts, caption rules, sponsor notes, athlete usage rules, clip examples, naming conventions, and review steps into company-owned files.

### **Athlete and NILP® Review**

Place athlete name, image, likeness, persona, voice, consent, usage scope, paid media, and reuse checks inside the production path.

### **Sponsor and Partner Approval Gates**

Define where sponsor, partner, league, club, brand, legal, and final delivery approval must happen.

### **Rights and Broadcast Review**

Build review points around broadcast rights, footage ownership, event access, distribution limits, territory, channel, format, and reuse.

### **Creation Rights Readiness**

Prepare the company flow, approval records, agent rules, athlete checks, sponsor review, rights checks, and internal process before Creation Rights carries the protected layer.

---

# **Section 3: Sports and Live Media Use Cases**

## **Eyebrow**

USE CASES

## **Headline**

Every live event creates more than one media path.

## **Body**

Creation Skills can support several common sports and live media production paths.

## **Use Case Cards**

### **Highlight Production**

Game clips, match moments, event highlights, play recaps, athlete moments, thumbnails, captions, and social cutdowns.

### **Athlete Content**

Athlete-led posts, training clips, interviews, behind-the-scenes moments, NILP® checks, sponsor usage, and repost permissions.

### **Sponsor and Partner Assets**

Branded recaps, sponsor packages, partner clips, activation materials, paid media assets, and campaign versions.

### **Live Event Recaps**

Event summaries, social packages, post-event videos, press materials, fan updates, internal reports, and partner-ready content.

### **Broadcast and Rights-Limited Clips**

Broadcast footage, rights-restricted clips, territory-limited materials, platform-specific edits, and partner delivery packages.

### **Team and League Social Media**

Captions, hooks, short-form edits, recap posts, story packages, platform versions, and approval records.

### **Archive and Season Refresh**

Historic clips, player milestones, season recaps, anniversary content, campaign refreshes, and reused event footage.

---

# **Section 4: Company-Owned Production Agents**

## **Eyebrow**

PRODUCTION AGENTS

## **Headline**

Sports agents need speed, limits, and approval paths.

## **Body**

Production agents can help sports and live media teams prepare content quickly.

They can summarize event notes, suggest highlights, draft captions, prepare sponsor versions, organize athlete content, build recap packages, and check delivery materials.

But they need rules before they support real sports media.

A production agent should know what it can prepare, what it cannot decide, who owns the output, and when a human must approve.

## **Example Agents**

### **Highlight Prep Agent**

Prepares clip suggestions, play notes, caption drafts, thumbnail options, and platform format notes.

### **Athlete Content Agent**

Prepares athlete post drafts, behind-the-scenes captions, usage reminders, and NILP® review flags.

### **Sponsor Asset Agent**

Prepares sponsor recap copy, branded content notes, partner versions, and delivery package materials.

### **Live Recap Agent**

Prepares event summaries, post-event social copy, fan updates, press notes, and internal recap materials.

### **Broadcast Clip Agent**

Prepares clip notes, rights flags, territory notes, and review materials for broadcast or platform-limited content.

### **Archive Refresh Agent**

Prepares historic clip summaries, player milestone content, season recap notes, and reuse review flags.

### **Internal Handoff Agent**

Prepares structured notes between production, social, brand, athlete relations, legal, sponsor, and delivery owners.

## **Agent Requirements**

Name  
Role  
Owner  
Department  
Approved tasks  
Blocked tasks  
Operating files  
Approved examples  
Tool access  
Token budget  
Review path  
Risk level  
Output format  
Audit history  
Last review date

---

# **Section 5: Operating Files for Sports Teams**

## **Eyebrow**

OPERATING FILES

## **Headline**

Live media rules should not live in scattered prompts.

## **Body**

Sports and live media teams often have strong production habits, but those habits may live across message threads, event notes, sponsor guidelines, athlete relations notes, platform specs, broadcast restrictions, reused prompts, and individual memory.

Creation Skills helps turn those instructions into operating files the company can own and maintain.

## **Operating Files May Define**

Agent role  
Approved tasks  
Blocked actions  
Approved examples  
Rejected examples  
Athlete usage rules  
Sponsor requirements  
Broadcast restrictions  
Caption structures  
Highlight formats  
Platform requirements  
Naming rules  
File handoff rules  
Review steps  
Escalation notes  
Owner instructions  
Risk level  
Last review date

## **Example**

A highlight prep operating file can define what the agent may suggest, what footage sources are approved, what sponsor or athlete review may be required, what cannot be posted, how outputs should be structured, and who approves the clip before it moves forward.

---

# **Section 6: Human Approval Gates**

## **Eyebrow**

HUMAN APPROVAL

## **Headline**

Live media should not move by assumption.

## **Body**

Creation Skills helps sports and live media teams define where human approval is required before media moves into public, paid, sponsor-facing, athlete-facing, partner-facing, reused, or final delivery contexts.

Production agents can prepare the work. People approve the moments that matter.

## **Approval Gates May Cover**

Final highlight clips  
Public posts  
Sponsor assets  
Partner-facing materials  
Athlete-facing materials  
Paid media assets  
Broadcast footage  
Rights-sensitive clips  
Archive reuse  
NILP® checks  
Talent usage  
Market versions  
Final delivery  
Escalations

## **Core Line**

The agent does the prep.  
The human makes the call.  
The system records both.

---

# **Section 7: Athlete, Talent, and NILP® Review**

## **Eyebrow**

ATHLETE AND NILP®

## **Headline**

Athlete-led media needs clear usage controls.

## **Body**

Sports media often includes athletes, coaches, performers, creators, fans, ambassadors, sponsors, voice, likeness, persona, behind-the-scenes footage, interviews, and third-party content.

That creates questions around name, image, likeness, persona, voice, consent, usage scope, channel, territory, duration, paid media, sponsor association, and reuse.

Creation Skills helps place those questions inside the production path.

## **Review Triggers**

Athlete likeness used  
Athlete name used  
Voice used  
Persona used  
NILP® present  
Interview material included  
Behind-the-scenes content included  
Sponsor association included  
Paid media planned  
New territory added  
New channel added  
Usage window extended  
Asset reused  
Asset localized  
Asset cut down  
Partner delivery planned  
Contract limits apply

## **Outcome**

Athlete-led and talent-led media moves with clearer review, approval, and recordkeeping.

---

# **Section 8: Sponsor and Partner Review**

## **Eyebrow**

SPONSOR AND PARTNER REVIEW

## **Headline**

Sponsor media needs approval before it moves.

## **Body**

Sports media often moves through sponsor, partner, league, club, event, or broadcaster obligations.

A highlight may become a sponsored post.  
A recap may include partner language.  
An athlete image may appear in a campaign.  
A live clip may move into paid media.  
A sponsor package may need a specific version.  
A territory may have distribution limits.

Creation Skills helps define the review points before sponsor or partner media moves forward.

## **Review Triggers**

Sponsor logo included  
Partner campaign included  
Athlete appears in sponsor asset  
Paid media planned  
Event clip reused  
Broadcast footage used  
League or club rule applies  
Territory restriction applies  
Platform-specific version created  
Partner-facing delivery planned  
Sponsor approval required  
Brand-sensitive wording included

## **Outcome**

Teams can produce sponsor and partner assets faster without relying on informal approval.

---

# **Section 9: Rights, Broadcast, and Reuse**

## **Eyebrow**

RIGHTS AND REUSE

## **Headline**

Live media needs rules before it gets reused.

## **Body**

Sports and live media assets are frequently reused.

A highlight becomes a paid ad.  
A player moment becomes a sponsor asset.  
A broadcast clip becomes a social post.  
A fan moment becomes an event recap.  
An archive clip becomes a season campaign.  
An interview becomes a partner package.

Creation Skills helps teams define when reuse is allowed, when review is required, and what needs to be recorded.

## **Reuse Questions**

What was the original approved use?

Who approved it?

What rights were granted?

Was athlete NILP® present?

Was sponsor association included?

Was broadcast footage included?

What channel was approved?

What territory was approved?

Was paid media included?

Was the usage window limited?

Has the asset changed?

Does the new use require league, club, sponsor, or athlete review?

What record should be kept?

## **Outcome**

Sports media teams can reuse assets with more confidence and less reliance on memory.

---

# **Section 10: Delivery Records**

## **Eyebrow**

DELIVERY RECORDS

## **Headline**

Finished sports media needs a clear record.

## **Body**

A final sports media package should not only include media files.

It should include enough context for the team to understand what was approved, what rights were checked, what version moved forward, and what record exists.

## **Delivery Record May Include**

Asset name  
Asset type  
Event or match reference  
Athlete or talent reference  
Sponsor or partner reference  
Owner  
Approver  
Approval date  
Rights status  
NILP® status  
Consent status  
Usage scope  
Territory  
Channel  
Format  
Version  
Source material notes  
Sponsor status  
Reuse status  
Escalation notes  
Final delivery status  
Audit reference

## **Outcome**

Delivery becomes easier to govern, reuse, and connect to Creation Rights where needed.

---

# **Section 11: System Repair for Sports and Live Media**

## **Eyebrow**

SYSTEM REPAIR

## **Headline**

Live production breaks in repeatable places.

## **Body**

Creation Skills helps sports and live media teams identify where production repeatedly breaks and design control points that route work back into order.

## **Common Breaks**

Final clip version is unclear.  
Sponsor approval arrives late.  
Athlete usage is unclear.  
Paid media rights are missing.  
Broadcast rights are not documented.  
Caption file is missing.  
Asset package is incomplete.  
Agent output exceeds approved task.  
Event brief changes after production starts.  
Reuse request lacks context.  
Delivery record is incomplete.  
Final post moves without approval.

## **Repair Actions**

Name the owner  
Add approval gate  
Move review earlier  
Update operating file  
Add blocked action  
Route to athlete, sponsor, legal, or rights review  
Clarify broadcast restriction  
Add NILP® review point  
Add sponsor version check  
Add delivery record requirement  
Prepare Creation Rights entry point

---

CREATION RIGHTS READINESS

Prepare the sports media process before the platform carries the record.

# **Section 13: Engagement Paths**

## **Eyebrow**

HOW WE HELP

## **Headline**

Start with one live production path, then scale what works.

## **Body**

Creation Skills helps sports and live media teams start with a focused review and build toward a stronger media production model.

## **Engagement Paths**

### **C/Skills Review**

Understand the current company flow, production-agent use, approval gaps, rights review points, athlete usage issues, sponsor review issues, delivery gaps, and Creation Rights readiness.

### **Operating Model Design**

Define the future model for how sports media moves through people, agents, approvals, athlete review, sponsor review, rights review, delivery, reuse, and records.

### **Agent Operating Blueprint**

Design production agents, operating files, prompt libraries, blocked actions, human checkpoints, and owner controls.

### **Governance Blueprint**

Define decision rights, approval gates, NILP® checks, rights review triggers, sponsor review points, athlete usage controls, reuse rules, escalation paths, and protected records.

### **Pilot Transformation**

Test the new model on one real event, highlight, sponsor, athlete, or recap production path.

### **Implementation Office**

Support rollout, owner guidance, agent supervision, operating-file updates, adoption, and Creation Rights readiness.

---

# **Section 14: What Good Looks Like**

## **Eyebrow**

OUTCOME

## **Headline**

A live media system the team can own and repeat.

## **Body**

After Creation Skills work, the sports or live media team should have a clearer model for how event media is prepared, reviewed, approved, delivered, reused, and recorded.

## **Good Looks Like**

Production paths are defined.  
Athlete review paths are clear.  
Sponsor review points are understood.  
Talent and NILP® checks are built in.  
Agent owners are named.  
Operating files are maintained.  
Rights review happens earlier.  
Delivery packages include stronger records.  
Reuse has a review path.  
Escalation has an owner.  
Creation Rights is ready to govern the protected layer.

# **Section 11: System Repair for Sports and Live Media**

## **Eyebrow**

SYSTEM REPAIR

## **Headline**

Live production breaks in repeatable places.

## **Body**

Creation Skills helps sports and live media teams identify where production repeatedly breaks and design control points that route work back into order.

## **Common Breaks**

Final clip version is unclear.  
Sponsor approval arrives late.  
Athlete usage is unclear.  
Paid media rights are missing.  
Broadcast rights are not documented.  
Caption file is missing.  
Asset package is incomplete.  
Agent output exceeds approved task.  
Event brief changes after production starts.  
Reuse request lacks context.  
Delivery record is incomplete.  
Final post moves without approval.

## **Repair Actions**

Name the owner  
Add approval gate  
Move review earlier  
Update operating file  
Add blocked action  
Route to athlete, sponsor, legal, or rights review  
Clarify broadcast restriction  
Add NILP® review point  
Add sponsor version check  
Add delivery record requirement  
Prepare Creation Rights entry point

NEWSROOMS AND PUBLISHERS

A story becomes headlines, images, captions, clips, newsletters, social posts, archive references, syndication packages, translations, audio versions, video cutdowns, and partner materials.

# **Section 1: The Editorial Production Problem**

## **Eyebrow**

THE PROBLEM

## **Headline**

Publishing now creates more media around every story.

## **Body**

Newsrooms and publishers are no longer only producing one article or one issue.

A story may need headlines, summaries, captions, image packages, social cutdowns, newsletters, homepage copy, search copy, push alerts, video scripts, audio notes, archive context, market versions, and syndication materials.

The production path can become scattered.

Source material changes.  
Headlines move quickly.  
Images need rights review.  
Archive material gets reused.  
Social posts need approval.  
Legal or standards review may be required.  
Agents may prepare summaries, captions, drafts, and packages.  
Records need to show what was approved before publication.

Creation Skills helps fix the company flow around editorial media.

---

# **Section 2: What We Help With**

## **Eyebrow**

WHAT WE HELP WITH

## **Headline**

People, agents, editorial review, rights, sources, and records in one company flow.

## **Body**

Creation Skills works with editorial, publishing, social, video, photo, archive, legal, standards, audience, and platform teams to clarify how media is prepared, reviewed, approved, published, reused, and recorded.

## **Support Areas**

### **Editorial Media Operating Model**

Define how stories, images, captions, clips, newsletters, archive references, social posts, and partner materials move from source to approval.

### **Company-Owned Production Agents**

Design production agents with defined jobs, owners, approved tasks, blocked actions, operating files, and human checkpoints.

### **Operating Files**

Turn scattered prompts, headline rules, style examples, caption formats, image requirements, archive notes, and review steps into company-owned files.

### **Editorial and Standards Approval Gates**

Define where editorial, legal, standards, photo, video, audience, social, archive, and final publication approval must happen.

### **Source and Rights Review**

Place source material, image rights, archive use, attribution, quotations, captions, third-party materials, and reuse checks inside the production path.

### **Archive and Syndication Controls**

Create rules for republishing, updating, translating, syndicating, packaging, or reusing editorial assets in new contexts.

### **Creation Rights Readiness**

Prepare the company flow, approval records, agent rules, source review, rights checks, metadata, and internal process before Creation Rights carries the protected layer.

---

# **Section 3: Newsroom and Publisher Use Cases**

## **Eyebrow**

USE CASES

## **Headline**

Every story creates more than one media path.

## **Body**

Creation Skills can support several common editorial and publishing production paths.

## **Use Case Cards**

### **Article and Feature Production**

Drafts, headlines, summaries, editor notes, image captions, source notes, legal review, and publication records.

### **Social and Audience Packages**

Social posts, headlines, captions, short clips, thumbnails, quote cards, newsletters, push alerts, and platform-specific versions.

### **Photo and Image Packages**

Image selections, captions, rights notes, attribution fields, alt text, metadata, approval notes, and final image packages.

### **Video and Audio Support**

Video summaries, transcript notes, caption drafts, podcast descriptions, clip suggestions, show notes, and delivery packages.

### **Archive and Evergreen Updates**

Older stories, archive images, explainers, timeline updates, anniversary packages, backgrounders, and republished material.

### **Syndication and Partner Materials**

Partner-ready copy, headline variants, image packages, licensing notes, translation materials, and publication records.

### **Newsletter and Membership Media**

Newsletter summaries, editorial notes, member updates, sponsor materials, curated links, and final approval paths.

---

# **Section 4: Company-Owned Production Agents**

## **Eyebrow**

PRODUCTION AGENTS

## **Headline**

Editorial agents need source context, limits, and approval paths.

## **Body**

Production agents can help publishing teams prepare editorial media faster.

They can summarize source materials, draft captions, prepare headline options, organize archive references, build newsletter summaries, clean transcripts, prepare social packages, and check delivery materials.

But they need rules before they support real editorial media.

A production agent should know what it can prepare, what it cannot decide, who owns the output, and when a human must approve.

## **Example Agents**

### **Story Summary Agent**

Prepares structured summaries, key points, source notes, and editor-ready briefing material from approved inputs.

### **Headline Options Agent**

Prepares headline options, dek options, social headlines, and search-friendly variations for editor review.

### **Image Caption Agent**

Prepares caption drafts, attribution notes, source references, rights flags, and alt text support.

### **Social Package Agent**

Prepares social copy, quote card options, short-form captions, platform notes, and audience package drafts.

### **Archive Context Agent**

Summarizes older material, flags source gaps, and prepares archive context for editor and rights review.

### **Transcript Cleanup Agent**

Prepares cleaned transcript drafts, speaker notes, timing references, and review-ready materials.

### **Newsletter Prep Agent**

Prepares newsletter summaries, subject line options, section notes, and member update drafts for human approval.

### **Partner Package Agent**

Prepares syndication notes, partner copy, image package requirements, metadata notes, and delivery checklists.

## **Agent Requirements**

Name  
Role  
Owner  
Department  
Approved tasks  
Blocked tasks  
Operating files  
Approved examples  
Tool access  
Token budget  
Review path  
Risk level  
Output format  
Audit history  
Last review date

---

# **Section 5: Operating Files for Editorial Teams**

## **Eyebrow**

OPERATING FILES

## **Headline**

Editorial rules should not live in scattered prompts.

## **Body**

Newsrooms and publishers often have strong editorial standards, but those standards may live across style guides, editor notes, image rules, legal notes, archive systems, message threads, reused prompts, and individual memory.

Creation Skills helps turn those instructions into operating files the company can own and maintain.

## **Operating Files May Define**

Agent role  
Approved tasks  
Blocked actions  
Approved examples  
Rejected examples  
Editorial style notes  
Headline rules  
Caption structures  
Attribution requirements  
Source requirements  
Archive rules  
Image rights triggers  
Social copy rules  
Newsletter format rules  
Naming rules  
Review steps  
Escalation notes  
Owner instructions  
Risk level  
Last review date

## **Example**

A headline options operating file can define what the agent may suggest, what tone is allowed, what language is blocked, which editorial standards apply, what requires editor review, and what cannot be published without human approval.

---

# **Section 6: Human Approval Gates**

## **Eyebrow**

HUMAN APPROVAL

## **Headline**

Editorial media should not move by assumption.

## **Body**

Creation Skills helps newsrooms and publishers define where human approval is required before media moves into public, partner-facing, syndicated, sponsored, archive-based, reused, or final publication contexts.

Production agents can prepare the work. People approve the moments that matter.

## **Approval Gates May Cover**

Final story copy  
Headlines  
Captions  
Image packages  
Public posts  
Newsletter sends  
Sponsored content  
Partner materials  
Final translations  
Archive reuse  
Source-sensitive material  
Rights-sensitive work  
Legal triggers  
Standards review  
Final publication  
Escalations

## **Core Line**

The agent does the prep.  
The human makes the call.  
The system records both.

---

# **Section 7: Source, Editorial, and Standards Review**

## **Eyebrow**

SOURCE AND STANDARDS REVIEW

## **Headline**

Source-sensitive media needs review before publication.

## **Body**

Editorial media often carries source, attribution, image, quotation, archive, legal, standards, and public-facing considerations.

Those questions should not appear only at final publication.

Creation Skills helps place review points earlier in the production path.

## **Review Triggers**

Source material included  
Quote included  
Third-party image included  
Archive material used  
Caption requires attribution  
Sponsored content included  
Sensitive subject matter included  
Correction or update required  
Public-facing headline created  
Partner publication planned  
Translation prepared  
Syndication planned  
Legal escalation required  
Standards review required

## **Outcome**

Teams know when media can move, when it must stop, and who needs to approve it.

---

# **Section 8: Image Rights, Captions, and Attribution**

## **Eyebrow**

IMAGE RIGHTS AND ATTRIBUTION

## **Headline**

Images need more than a caption.

## **Body**

Published image packages often need source details, photographer credit, rights status, caption approval, alt text, metadata, archive context, usage notes, and reuse limits.

Creation Skills helps publishers design the approval path around images before they move into stories, newsletters, social, partner packages, or archive reuse.

## **Image Review Questions**

What is the source image?

Who owns it?

What rights were granted?

Is attribution required?

Is the image being reused?

Is it moving into a new channel?

Is it being cropped, edited, or repackaged?

Is a person identifiable?

Is NILP® present?

Is the caption approved?

Is alt text required?

What record should be kept?

## **Outcome**

Image packages move with clearer source, rights, caption, accessibility, and reuse records.

---

# **Section 9: Archive, Reuse, and Updates**

## **Eyebrow**

ARCHIVE AND REUSE

## **Headline**

Published media needs a rule set before new use.

## **Body**

Editorial assets rarely stay in one place.

A story becomes an explainer.  
A quote becomes a social card.  
An image becomes a newsletter asset.  
An archive story becomes a timeline.  
A video clip becomes a partner package.  
A feature becomes an evergreen update.

Creation Skills helps publishers define when reuse is allowed, when review is required, and what needs to be recorded.

## **Reuse Questions**

What was the original approved use?

Who approved it?

What rights were granted?

Was image rights review completed?

Was a person identifiable?

Was NILP® present?

Was the asset edited or reframed?

Is the story being updated?

Is the asset being syndicated?

Is the asset being translated?

Does the new use require additional approval?

What record should be kept?

## **Outcome**

Editorial teams can reuse and update media with stronger context and less reliance on memory.

---

# **Section 10: Delivery and Publication Records**

## **Eyebrow**

PUBLICATION RECORDS

## **Headline**

Published media should leave a clear record.

## **Body**

A final editorial package should not only include copy and files.

It should include enough context for the company to understand what was approved, what source material was used, what rights were checked, what version moved forward, and what record exists.

## **Publication Record May Include**

Asset name  
Asset type  
Story or project reference  
Owner  
Editor  
Approver  
Approval date  
Source status  
Rights status  
NILP® status where relevant  
Attribution status  
Consent status where relevant  
Usage scope  
Channel  
Format  
Version  
Image package status  
Caption status  
Translation status  
Archive status  
Reuse status  
Escalation notes  
Publication status  
Audit reference

## **Outcome**

Publication becomes easier to govern, update, reuse, and connect to Creation Rights where needed.

---

# **Section 1: The Talent-Led Media Problem**

## **Eyebrow**

THE PROBLEM

## **Headline**

Talent-led assets often outgrow their original approval.

## **Body**

Creator and talent-led assets rarely stay in one place.

A creator video is reposted.  
A model image is used in a new market.  
An athlete highlight becomes paid media.  
An artist clip becomes partner content.  
An employee video becomes a recruitment asset.  
A customer testimonial becomes a product page.  
A behind-the-scenes asset becomes campaign material.

The original approval may not cover every new use.

That creates questions.

Who approved the talent usage?  
Was NILP® present?  
Was consent captured?  
Was paid media allowed?  
What territory was approved?  
What channel was approved?  
What was the usage window?  
Can the asset be reused?  
Can it be localized?  
Can an agent prepare new versions?  
What should Creation Rights govern?

Creation Skills helps teams place those questions inside the production path before media moves forward.

---

# **Section 2: What We Help With**

## **Eyebrow**

WHAT WE HELP WITH

## **Headline**

People, agents, talent approvals, NILP®, usage, and records in one company flow.

## **Body**

Creation Skills works with teams managing creator, influencer, ambassador, athlete, artist, model, performer, employee, and talent-led media to clarify how assets are prepared, reviewed, approved, delivered, reused, and recorded.

### **Talent-Led Media Operating Model**

Define how creator deliverables, talent assets, campaign materials, paid media, reposts, market versions, and reuse requests move from brief to approval.

### **Company-Owned Production Agents**

Design production agents with defined jobs, owners, approved tasks, blocked actions, operating files, and human checkpoints.

### **Operating Files**

Turn scattered prompts, creator briefs, approval notes, contract reminders, usage rules, naming conventions, and review steps into company-owned files.

### **NILP® and Consent Review**

Place name, image, likeness, persona, voice, consent, usage scope, territory, channel, format, paid media, and reuse checks inside the production path.

### **Talent Approval Gates**

Define where creator, talent, management, brand, legal, paid media, partner, and final delivery approval must happen.

### **Reuse and Paid Media Controls**

Create rules for when talent-led assets can be refreshed, cut down, reposted, localized, placed into paid media, or moved into another channel.

### **Creation Rights Readiness**

Prepare the company flow, approval records, agent rules, NILP® checks, usage controls, and internal process before Creation Rights carries the protected layer.

# **Section 4: Company-Owned Production Agents**

## **Eyebrow**

PRODUCTION AGENTS

## **Headline**

Talent media agents need clear limits before they touch the work.

## **Body**

Production agents can help teams prepare creator and talent-led media faster.

They can summarize creator submissions, prepare caption drafts, check delivery packages, flag missing usage details, organize repost requests, prepare paid media versions, and draft market adaptations.

But they need rules before they support talent-led assets.

A production agent should know what it can prepare, what it cannot decide, who owns the output, and when a human must approve.

## **Example Agents**

### **Creator Submission Agent**

Prepares creator submission summaries, asset notes, missing item flags, and review-ready materials.

### **Talent Usage Check Agent**

Flags whether name, image, likeness, persona, voice, consent, paid media, territory, channel, or usage window review may be required.

### **Repost Prep Agent**

Prepares repost copy, source notes, permission reminders, and approval materials before brand use.

### **Paid Media Version Agent**

Prepares channel versions, caption drafts, format notes, usage reminders, and review flags before paid placement.

### **Market Version Agent**

Prepares localized copy drafts, territory notes, usage reminders, and approval materials.

### **Creator Delivery Agent**

Checks whether deliverables include required files, captions, thumbnails, metadata, usage notes, and approval records.

### **Talent Campaign Agent**

Prepares campaign asset summaries, talent-facing notes, caption drafts, and delivery package materials.

Talent-led media often depends on details that may be spread across briefs, contracts, emails, creator guidelines, agency notes, platform requirements, brand rules, and individual memory.

Territory rules  
Channel rules  
Usage-window notes  
Caption structures  
Naming rules  
File handoff rules  
Review steps  
Escalation notes  
Owner instructions  
Risk level  
Last review date

## **Example**

A creator deliverable operating file can define what the agent may prepare, which deliverables are expected, what usage details must be checked, what cannot be assumed, and who approves the asset before repost, paid media, market adaptation, or final delivery.

---

# **Section 6: Human Approval Gates**

## **Eyebrow**

HUMAN APPROVAL

## **Headline**

Talent-led media should not move by assumption.

## **Body**

Creation Skills helps teams define where human approval is required before talent-led media moves into public, paid, partner-facing, sponsor-facing, client-facing, reused, localized, or final delivery contexts.

Production agents can prepare the work. People approve the moments that matter.

## **Approval Gates May Cover**

Creator deliverables  
Influencer submissions  
Talent-facing materials  
Public posts  
Paid media assets  
Sponsor assets  
Partner-facing materials  
Client-facing materials  
Reposts  
Final translations  
Market versions  
Voice or likeness use  
NILP® checks  
Consent review  
Reuse requests  
Final delivery  
Escalations

## **Core Line**

The agent does the prep.  
The human makes the call.  
The system records both.

---

# **Section 7: NILP® and Consent Review**

## **Eyebrow**

NILP® AND CONSENT

## **Headline**

Identity-sensitive media needs review before it moves.

## **Body**

Talent-led media may include name, image, likeness, persona, voice, signature style, performance, creator content, behind-the-scenes material, testimonials, interviews, or reposted social media.

Creation Skills helps teams define when NILP®, consent, talent, legal, or rights review should happen before media moves forward.

## **Review Triggers**

Name used  
Image used  
Likeness used  
Persona used  
Voice used  
Creator content included  
Talent-facing material created  
Behind-the-scenes content included  
Testimonial used  
Paid media planned  
Sponsor association included  
New channel added  
New territory added  
Usage window extended  
Asset reused  
Asset localized  
Asset cut down  
Contract limit may apply

## **Outcome**

Talent-led media moves with clearer review, approval, and recordkeeping.

---

# **Section 8: Paid Media and Reuse Controls**

## **Eyebrow**

PAID MEDIA AND REUSE

## **Headline**

A post is not always approved for every future use.

## **Body**

One of the biggest risks in talent-led media is reuse.

An organic post becomes a paid ad.  
A creator video becomes a product page asset.  
A model image becomes a market version.  
An athlete clip becomes sponsor content.  
A customer testimonial becomes campaign media.  
A behind-the-scenes asset becomes public-facing content.

Creation Skills helps teams define when reuse is allowed, when review is required, and what record should exist.

## **Reuse Questions**

What was the original approved use?

Who approved it?

Was paid media included?

Was organic use only?

What territory was approved?

What channel was approved?

Was the usage window limited?

Was the talent or creator identifiable?

Was NILP® present?

Was consent captured?

Has the asset been edited?

Has the context changed?

Does the new use require additional approval?

What record should be kept?

## **Outcome**

Teams can reuse talent-led assets with stronger controls and less reliance on assumption.

---

# **Section 9: Contract and Usage Awareness**

## **Eyebrow**

USAGE AWARENESS

## **Headline**

Teams need to know when usage terms matter.

## **Body**

Creation Skills does not replace legal counsel.

But teams need enough operating awareness to know when an asset should stop for review.

Talent-led media often depends on usage terms that affect where, when, and how an asset can move.

## **Usage Terms That May Matter**

Duration  
Territory  
Channel  
Format  
Paid media  
Organic social  
Whitelisting  
Dark posts  
Reposts  
Exclusivity  
Brand category  
Sponsor association  
Market adaptation  
Localization  
Archive use  
Revocation or approval rights

## **Outcome**

Teams can recognize when a usage question needs review before media moves too far.

---

# **Section 10: Delivery Records**

## **Eyebrow**

DELIVERY RECORDS

## **Headline**

Talent-led media needs a clear record.

## **Body**

A final talent-led media package should not only include files.

It should include enough context for the company to understand what was approved, what usage was checked, what version moved forward, and what record exists.

## **Delivery Record May Include**

Asset name  
Asset type  
Talent or creator reference  
Campaign reference  
Owner  
Approver  
Approval date  
NILP® status  
Consent status  
Usage scope  
Paid media status  
Territory  
Channel  
Format  
Version  
Source material notes  
Contract or usage notes  
Reuse status  
Escalation notes  
Final delivery status  
Audit reference

## **Outcome**

Delivery becomes easier to govern, reuse, and connect to Creation Rights where needed.

---

# **Section 11: System Repair for Talent-Led Media**

## **Eyebrow**

SYSTEM REPAIR

## **Headline**

Talent-led production breaks in repeatable places.

## **Body**

Creation Skills helps teams identify where talent-led media repeatedly breaks and design control points that route work back into order.

## **Common Breaks**

Creator deliverable is incomplete.  
Usage terms are unclear.  
Paid media rights are missing.  
Repost permission is assumed.  
Talent approval arrives late.  
NILP® check is missing.  
Contract limit is not visible.  
Market version lacks review.  
Agent output exceeds approved task.  
Asset is reused without context.  
Final delivery record is incomplete.

## **Repair Actions**

Name the owner  
Add approval gate  
Move review earlier  
Update operating file  
Add blocked action  
Route to talent, legal, brand, or rights review  
Clarify paid media rule  
Add NILP® review point  
Add reuse check  
Add delivery record requirement  
Prepare Creation Rights entry point

---

# **Hero**

## **Eyebrow**

LEGAL, RIGHTS, AND RISK TEAMS

## **Headline**

For teams responsible for what can move forward.

## **Subhead**

Creation Skills helps legal, rights, compliance, risk, and governance teams place review, approval, NILP® checks, reuse controls, and records inside the media creation process.

## **Body**

Legal and rights teams are often pulled in too late.

The asset is already cut.  
The campaign is already moving.  
The talent appears in the edit.  
The creator asset is being reused.  
The product claim is already in copy.  
The production agent has already prepared versions.  
The team wants to publish, deliver, or boost.

Creation Skills helps legal, rights, and risk teams move from late-stage review to earlier operating control.

The goal is not to slow creative production. The goal is to define where review belongs, what agents can prepare, what people must approve, what records need to exist, and what Creation Rights should govern.

## **Primary CTA**

Book a C/Skills Review

## **Secondary CTA**

Explore Engagements

---

# **Section 1: The Legal and Rights Problem**

## **Eyebrow**

THE PROBLEM

## **Headline**

Rights review often arrives after the work is already made.

## **Body**

Modern media production creates more rights-sensitive moments than older approval models were built to handle.

A social cutdown includes talent.  
A creator post becomes paid media.  
A product claim appears in a caption.  
A voice clip gets reused.  
An archive asset enters a campaign.  
A market version changes the context.  
A production agent prepares new copy from old material.  
A team wants to reuse an approved asset in a new channel.

Legal and rights teams are then asked to make fast decisions with incomplete context.

Creation Skills helps move the review points earlier, so the company knows when media can move, when it must stop, and who needs to approve it.

---

# **Section 2: What We Help With**

## **Eyebrow**

WHAT WE HELP WITH

## **Headline**

Rights review, agent limits, approval gates, and records in one company flow.

## **Body**

Creation Skills works with legal, rights, compliance, risk, and governance teams to define how media should be reviewed, approved, reused, escalated, and recorded.

## **Support Areas**

### **Rights Review Points**

Define where rights, source material, usage scope, territory, channel, format, duration, and reuse checks belong inside the production path.

### **NILP® and Talent Checks**

Define when name, image, likeness, persona, voice, consent, talent usage, creator content, or performer materials need review.

### **Production-Agent Limits**

Define what agents can prepare, what they cannot decide, and when outputs must stop for legal, rights, brand, talent, or risk review.

### **Human Approval Gates**

Define where people approve public, paid, client-facing, talent-facing, reused, rights-sensitive, or final media.

### **Operating Files**

Turn legal review triggers, blocked actions, usage rules, escalation notes, and approval requirements into company-owned files agents and teams can follow.

### **Reuse Controls**

Create rules for when approved assets can be cut down, localized, repackaged, translated, boosted, revived, or used in a new context.

### **Creation Rights Readiness**

Prepare the approval paths, record requirements, rights checks, and operating rules needed before Creation Rights carries the protected layer.

---

# **Section 3: Legal and Rights Use Cases**

## **Eyebrow**

USE CASES

## **Headline**

Rights-sensitive media appears across every production path.

## **Body**

Creation Skills can support several legal, rights, and risk review areas.

## **Use Case Cards**

### **Talent and NILP® Review**

Name, image, likeness, persona, voice, consent, usage scope, approval rights, paid media, territory, duration, and reuse.

### **Creator and Influencer Assets**

Creator deliverables, reposts, whitelisting, paid usage, platform versions, usage windows, and brand reuse.

### **Product Claims**

Performance claims, comparison claims, health or beauty claims, technical claims, sustainability claims, pricing claims, and offer language.

### **Archive and Source Material**

Third-party images, clips, footage, music, archive references, captions, attributions, source notes, and reuse context.

### **Paid Media and Boosting**

Organic assets moving into paid media, dark posts, whitelisting, sponsor assets, influencer usage, and channel expansion.

### **Market and Territory Versions**

Localized copy, new territories, channel changes, language adaptations, regional requirements, and market-specific review.

### **Production-Agent Outputs**

Agent-prepared captions, translations, summaries, cuts, versions, claims, reuse suggestions, and review packages.

### **Final Delivery Records**

Approval records, rights status, NILP® status, consent status, usage scope, metadata, delivery status, and audit references.

---

# **Section 4: Company-Owned Production Agents**

## **Eyebrow**

PRODUCTION AGENTS

## **Headline**

Agents need legal and rights limits before they support real media.

## **Body**

Production agents can help teams prepare material faster, but they should not make rights decisions.

They can draft, summarize, translate, format, flag, package, and prepare.

They should not approve talent use, change rights terms, clear product claims, reuse restricted assets, approve NILP® usage, or decide what can be published.

Creation Skills helps legal and rights teams define the limits.

## **Example Agents**

### **Rights Review Prep Agent**

Flags possible rights, NILP®, talent, usage, territory, channel, format, or reuse questions before review.

### **Talent Usage Check Agent**

Identifies when a person’s name, image, likeness, persona, or voice may require approval.

### **Product Claim Flag Agent**

Flags product, performance, comparison, sustainability, health, beauty, financial, or technical claims for review.

### **Reuse Review Prep Agent**

Prepares original approval context, usage scope, territory, channel, talent status, and rights notes before reuse.

### **Contract Reminder Agent**

Summarizes approved usage terms, known restrictions, approval requirements, and escalation notes from approved company materials.

### **Delivery Record Agent**

Checks whether approval, rights, NILP®, consent, metadata, version, and delivery records appear complete before final movement.

## **Agent Requirements**

Name  
Role  
Owner  
Department  
Approved tasks  
Blocked tasks  
Operating files  
Approved examples  
Tool access  
Token budget  
Review path  
Risk level  
Output format  
Audit history  
Last review date

---

# **Section 5: Operating Files for Legal and Rights Teams**

## **Eyebrow**

OPERATING FILES

## **Headline**

Review rules should not live only in policy documents.

## **Body**

Legal and rights guidance often sits in contracts, policies, spreadsheets, email threads, approval notes, outside counsel comments, and individual memory.

Creation Skills helps turn review triggers and blocked actions into operating files that teams and production agents can actually use.

## **Operating Files May Define**

Agent role  
Approved tasks  
Blocked actions  
Rights review triggers  
NILP® review triggers  
Consent review triggers  
Talent usage rules  
Paid media rules  
Reuse rules  
Territory limits  
Channel limits  
Usage-window reminders  
Claim review triggers  
Escalation notes  
Owner instructions  
Risk level  
Last review date

## **Example**

A talent usage operating file can define what the agent may flag, what cannot be assumed, when NILP® review is required, when paid media creates additional review, and who must approve before the asset moves forward.

## **Eyebrow**

HUMAN APPROVAL

## **Headline**

## **Body**

Creation Skills helps legal and rights teams define where human approval is required before media moves into public, paid, client-facing, partner-facing, talent-facing, reused, localized, or final delivery contexts.

Production agents can prepare the work. People approve the moments that matter.

The system records both.

# **Section 7: NILP® and Talent Governance**

## **Eyebrow**

NILP® AND TALENT

## **Headline**

Identity-sensitive media needs earlier review.

## **Body**

Name, image, likeness, persona, and voice can appear in many places before a final asset is approved.

A creator appears in a product video.  
An athlete appears in a sponsor clip.  
An artist appears in a campaign cutdown.  
A model image moves into paid media.  
A voice clip is reused.  
A behind-the-scenes asset becomes public content.

Creation Skills helps legal and rights teams define when those moments should trigger review.

## **Review Triggers**

Name used  
Image used  
Likeness used  
Persona used  
Voice used  
Talent identifiable  
Creator content included  
Behind-the-scenes content included  
Paid media planned  
Sponsor association included  
New channel added  
New territory added  
Usage window extended  
Asset reused  
Asset localized  
Asset cut down  
Contract limit may apply

## **Outcome**

Teams know when NILP®, consent, legal, talent, or rights review is required before media moves too far.

---

# **Section 8: Product Claims and Risk Review**

## **Eyebrow**

CLAIMS AND RISK

## **Headline**

Claims need review before they become media.

## **Body**

Claims can appear in many forms: captions, scripts, PDP copy, marketplace copy, ads, creator videos, subtitles, pitch materials, social posts, and product pages.

Creation Skills helps define when claim review belongs inside the production path.

## **Claim Review Triggers**

Performance claim  
Comparison claim  
Product benefit claim  
Health or beauty claim  
Financial claim  
Technical claim  
Sustainability claim  
Material claim  
Offer or pricing claim  
Before-and-after claim  
Creator endorsement  
Marketplace copy  
Paid media use  
Legal-sensitive wording

## **Outcome**

The company knows when brand, product, legal, compliance, or risk review is required before publication or delivery.

---

# **Section 9: Asset Reuse and Usage Scope**

## **Eyebrow**

REUSE AND USAGE

## **Headline**

Reuse should not depend on memory.

## **Body**

Most rights problems appear when an asset moves into a new use.

An organic creator post becomes a paid ad.  
A campaign image moves into a new territory.  
A product video becomes marketplace content.  
A broadcast clip becomes social media.  
A model image becomes a retail asset.  
An archive photo becomes a campaign reference.

Creation Skills helps define the reuse questions teams should answer before the asset moves.

## **Reuse Questions**

What was the original approved use?

Who approved it?

What rights were granted?

Was NILP® present?

Was consent captured?

What territory was approved?

What channel was approved?

What format was approved?

Was paid media included?

Was the usage window limited?

Has the asset changed?

Has the context changed?

Does the new use require additional approval?

What record should be kept?

## **Outcome**

Reuse becomes a controlled decision, not an assumption.

---

# **Section 10: Records and Evidence**

## **Eyebrow**

RECORDS

## **Headline**

Approval should leave a record the company can trust.

## **Body**

Legal and rights teams need more than verbal approval or scattered message threads.

Creation Skills helps define what information should be captured before media moves into public, paid, licensed, reused, client-facing, partner-facing, or final delivery contexts.

## **Record May Include**

Asset name  
Asset type  
Owner  
Approver  
Approval date  
Rights status  
NILP® status  
Consent status  
Talent usage status  
Usage scope  
Territory  
Channel  
Format  
Version  
Source material notes  
Claim review status  
Reuse status  
Escalation notes  
Final delivery status  
Audit reference

## **Creation Rights Connection**

Creation Rights can carry the protected record around rights, NILP®, consent, approvals, metadata, audit, payouts, and compliance-sensitive media activity.

Creation Skills prepares the company process so those records are meaningful.

---

# **Section 1: The Platform Problem**

## **Eyebrow**

THE PROBLEM

## **Headline**

Systems cannot fix a company process that has not been defined.

## **Body**

Most enterprises already have tools.

They may have a DAM.  
They may have a MAM.  
They may have project tools.  
They may have review tools.  
They may have AI tools.  
They may have creative suites.  
They may have storage, archives, metadata, access controls, and delivery systems.

But the hard question remains:

What should each system know?  
Who owns the asset?  
Which version is approved?  
Which agent prepared the output?  
Who reviewed it?  
What rights apply?  
Can it be reused?  
What metadata is required?  
When should Creation Rights govern the protected record?

Creation Skills helps define the operating layer before systems are expected to carry it.

---

# **Section 2: What We Help With**

## **Eyebrow**

WHAT WE HELP WITH

## **Headline**

People, systems, agents, approvals, metadata, and records in one company flow.

## **Body**

Creation Skills works with platform, technology, DAM, MAM, data, and operations teams to clarify how media systems should support production, approval, rights review, reuse, and recordkeeping.

## **Support Areas**

### **Platform Readiness**

Prepare the company process, roles, approval points, metadata, and records needed before Creation Rights or other systems carry protected media activity.

### **Digital Asset Structure**

Define how assets should be named, versioned, described, approved, reused, archived, retired, and connected to records.

### **Production-Agent Governance**

Design how company-owned production agents access files, follow operating files, stay inside limits, and route outputs for human review.

### **Metadata and Record Requirements**

Define the fields, statuses, references, approval notes, rights notes, NILP® status, consent status, and reuse context systems need to carry.

### **Access and Permission Rules**

Clarify who can view, edit, approve, reuse, export, publish, or send media into protected systems.

### **Approval Gate Integration**

Define where human approval happens and what record should be created before media moves forward.

### **Creation Rights Readiness**

Prepare the company flow, asset data, approval records, agent rules, and protected media records before Creation Rights adoption.

---

# **Section 3: Platform and Digital Asset Use Cases**

## **Eyebrow**

USE CASES

## **Headline**

Every system needs a clear role in the media process.

## **Body**

Creation Skills can support several common platform and digital asset needs.

## **Use Case Cards**

### **DAM and MAM Readiness**

Asset naming, version control, metadata fields, usage notes, approval status, source material, and reuse context.

### **Creation Rights Adoption**

Process readiness, protected records, approval gates, rights review points, NILP® checks, and metadata requirements before platform use.

### **Production-Agent Access**

Agent roles, file access, tool permissions, approved examples, blocked actions, token limits, and review paths.

### **Asset Lifecycle Design**

Rules for asset creation, versioning, localization, refresh, reuse, retirement, archive, and final delivery.

### **Metadata and Record Model**

Fields for approval, rights, NILP®, consent, talent usage, usage scope, territory, channel, format, and audit references.

### **Review and Approval Systems**

How review tools, approval gates, human decisions, and Creation Rights records should connect.

### **Integration Planning**

Clarifying what information needs to move between creative tools, DAM, MAM, review tools, agent systems, and Creation Rights.

---

# **Section 4: Company-Owned Production Agents**

## **Eyebrow**

PRODUCTION AGENTS

## **Headline**

Agents need system rules before they access company media.

## **Body**

Production agents can support media production, but platform teams need to know how those agents should access systems, files, metadata, and records.

A production agent should not have open-ended access by default.

It needs a defined role, owner, approved tasks, blocked actions, operating files, access limits, review path, and risk level.

## **Example Agents**

### **Asset Package Agent**

Checks whether final packages include required files, formats, captions, metadata, approval notes, and delivery materials.

### **Metadata Prep Agent**

Prepares draft metadata, naming suggestions, source references, usage notes, and missing-field flags for human review.

### **Reuse Review Prep Agent**

Flags original approval, usage scope, territory, channel, talent status, NILP® status, and rights questions before reuse.

### **Delivery Checklist Agent**

Checks delivery readiness, required formats, captions, subtitles, naming rules, approval status, and record completeness.

### **Archive Summary Agent**

Summarizes archive materials and flags missing source, rights, metadata, or reuse information before material moves forward.

### **Internal Handoff Agent**

Prepares structured handoff notes between creative, production, legal, brand, platform, DAM, MAM, and delivery owners.

## **Agent Requirements**

Name  
Role  
Owner  
Department  
Approved tasks  
Blocked tasks  
Operating files  
Approved examples  
Tool access  
Token budget  
Review path  
Risk level  
Output format  
Audit history  
Last review date

---

# **Section 5: Operating Files for Platform Teams**

## **Eyebrow**

OPERATING FILES

## **Headline**

System rules should not live in scattered documents.

## **Body**

Technology and platform teams often receive requirements from many places: creative teams, legal, brand, asset managers, production teams, vendors, AI tool owners, and leadership.

Creation Skills helps turn those requirements into operating files that agents and teams can follow.

## **Operating Files May Define**

Agent role  
Approved tasks  
Blocked actions  
File access rules  
Tool access rules  
Approved examples  
Metadata requirements  
Naming rules  
Versioning rules  
Asset handoff rules  
Review steps  
Approval gates  
Escalation notes  
Owner instructions  
Risk level  
Last review date

## **Example**

A metadata preparation operating file can define which fields the agent may suggest, which fields require human confirmation, what source material must be referenced, what rights fields cannot be assumed, and who approves metadata before the asset moves into a protected record.

---

# **Section 6: Access and Permissions**

## **Eyebrow**

ACCESS AND PERMISSIONS

## **Headline**

Agent access should be designed, not assumed.

## **Body**

Production agents may need access to creative briefs, asset packages, reference files, approved examples, metadata fields, transcripts, captions, and delivery requirements.

But access should match the agent’s job.

Creation Skills helps platform teams define what each production agent can see, prepare, suggest, export, or route.

## **Access Questions**

What files can the agent access?

What systems can the agent touch?

Can the agent read metadata?

Can the agent suggest metadata?

Can the agent create new assets?

Can the agent export files?

Can the agent access talent materials?

Can the agent access rights notes?

Can the agent access unreleased assets?

Can the agent prepare public-facing outputs?

Who reviews the output before it moves?

What record should be kept?

## **Outcome**

Agents can support production without creating uncontrolled access to media, records, or sensitive materials.

---

# **Section 7: Human Approval Gates**

## **Eyebrow**

HUMAN APPROVAL

## **Headline**

Systems should reflect human decisions.

## **Body**

Technology can route work, but people still need to approve the moments that matter.

Creation Skills helps platform and digital asset teams define where human approval should be captured before media moves into public, paid, client-facing, partner-facing, reused, licensed, or final delivery contexts.

## **Approval Gates May Cover**

Final creative  
Metadata approval  
Rights-sensitive work  
NILP® checks  
Talent usage  
Consent review  
Product claims  
Paid media assets  
Archive reuse  
Final translations  
Market versions  
Client-facing materials  
Partner delivery  
Final delivery  
Escalations

## **Core Line**

The agent does the prep.  
The human makes the call.  
The system records both.

---

# **Section 8: Metadata and Records**

## **Eyebrow**

METADATA AND RECORDS

## **Headline**

A record is only useful if the company knows what it needs to capture.

## **Body**

Platform teams often inherit incomplete metadata because the company process did not define what needed to be captured earlier.

Creation Skills helps define what information should be captured before media moves into DAM, MAM, delivery systems, or Creation Rights.

## **Record Fields May Include**

Asset name  
Asset type  
Owner  
Production path  
Approver  
Approval date  
Rights status  
NILP® status  
Consent status  
Talent usage status  
Usage scope  
Territory  
Channel  
Format  
Version  
Source material notes  
Claim review status  
Reuse status  
Delivery status  
Escalation notes  
Audit reference

## **Outcome**

Systems can carry better records because the company process defines what those records mean.

---

# **Section 9: Asset Lifecycle and Reuse**

## **Eyebrow**

ASSET LIFECYCLE

## **Headline**

Digital assets need rules after first delivery.

## **Body**

Assets rarely stop after delivery.

They are reused, localized, refreshed, cut down, repackaged, translated, archived, revived, placed into paid media, added to product pages, sent to partners, or used in new campaigns.

Technology teams need a clear rule set for when assets can move again.

## **Lifecycle Questions**

Where did the asset originate?

Who owns it?

Who approved it?

What rights apply?

Was NILP® present?

Was consent captured?

What usage scope was approved?

What territory was approved?

What channel was approved?

What format was approved?

Was paid media included?

Was the usage window limited?

Has the asset changed?

Is the asset retired?

Does the new use need review?

What record should be updated?

## **Outcome**

Asset lifecycle rules make reuse clearer, safer, and easier to connect to Creation Rights.

---

# **Section 10: System Repair for Platform Teams**

## **Eyebrow**

SYSTEM REPAIR

## **Headline**

Platform problems often begin as process gaps.

## **Body**

Creation Skills helps technology and digital asset teams identify where production breaks before the issue becomes a systems problem.

## **Common Breaks**

Metadata is missing.  
Asset owner is unclear.  
Final version is unclear.  
Rights status is incomplete.  
NILP® status is missing.  
Approval record is not captured.  
Agent output exceeds approved task.  
Asset package is incomplete.  
Reuse request lacks context.  
File naming is inconsistent.  
Delivery status is unclear.  
Platform entry is not ready.

## **Repair Actions**

Name the owner  
Add approval gate  
Define metadata fields  
Update operating file  
Add blocked action  
Clarify asset lifecycle rule  
Add reuse review point  
Add delivery record requirement  
Define Creation Rights entry point  
Prepare platform handoff

---

# **Section 11: Creation Rights Readiness**

## **Eyebrow**

CREATION RIGHTS READINESS

## **Headline**

Prepare the operating layer before the platform carries the record.

## **Body**

Creation Skills helps technology, platform, DAM, MAM, and digital asset teams prepare the operating model, production-agent rules, operating files, human approval gates, metadata requirements, rights review points, asset lifecycle rules, reuse controls, and record model needed before and during Creation Rights adoption.

Creation Rights provides the platform layer for rights, NILP®, consent, talent use, metadata, approvals, audit, payouts, and compliance-sensitive media records.

HOW WE HELP

Creation Skills helps technology and platform teams start with a focused review and build toward a stronger operating layer for media systems.

Production paths are defined.

# **Section 1: The Leadership Problem**

## **Eyebrow**

THE PROBLEM

## **Headline**

Media production has become an enterprise operating issue.

## **Body**

Most leadership teams can see the shift.

Creative teams are moving faster.  
Marketing wants more versions.  
Legal wants earlier review.  
Technology wants cleaner requirements.  
Production wants fewer broken handoffs.  
Talent teams need clearer usage controls.  
Finance and risk want better records.  
The business wants modern media output without losing control.

The challenge is that each department often sees only part of the problem.

Creation Skills helps leadership define the full company system around media creation: people, production agents, operating files, approval gates, rights review, asset reuse, records, and Creation Rights readiness.

---

# **Section 2: What Leaders Need to Decide**

## **Eyebrow**

LEADERSHIP DECISIONS

## **Headline**

The company needs clear decisions before the system scales.

## **Body**

Creation Skills helps leadership answer the questions that sit above individual tools or team-level process.

## **Leadership Questions**

What media creation model does the company need now?

Which production paths matter most?

Where can company-owned agents support the work?

Where must people approve?

Who owns rights-sensitive decisions?

How should NILP®, consent, and talent usage be reviewed?

What assets can be reused?

What records must exist before delivery?

What belongs in Creation Rights?

What skills do teams need to operate the new model?

What should be piloted before wider rollout?

---

# **Section 3: What We Help With**

## **Eyebrow**

WHAT WE HELP WITH

## **Headline**

A leadership-ready model for people, agents, approvals, rights, and records.

## **Body**

Creation Skills works with executive sponsors to define the operating layer behind modern media creation.

## **Support Areas**

### **Enterprise Media Operating Model**

Define how creative work should move across teams, production paths, approval gates, rights review, delivery, reuse, and records.

### **Company-Owned Production Agents**

Create a leadership view of where agents can support production, who owns them, what they can do, and what they cannot decide.

### **Human Approval Gates**

Define where human judgment is required before media moves into public, paid, client-facing, partner-facing, talent-facing, reused, licensed, or final delivery contexts.

### **Rights and NILP® Governance**

Place rights, consent, talent use, NILP®, usage scope, territory, channel, format, and reuse review into the company process.

### **Operating Files**

Turn scattered prompts, examples, rules, review steps, and blocked actions into company-owned files teams can maintain.

### **Creation Rights Readiness**

Prepare the company process, records, owners, approval paths, and protected media activity before Creation Rights carries the platform layer.

### **Change and Adoption**

Help teams understand what changed, who owns what, and how the new model becomes part of normal production.

---

# **Section 4: Executive Stakeholders**

## **Eyebrow**

WHO THIS SUPPORTS

## **Headline**

For leaders who need the company system to work.

## **Body**

Creation Skills supports the leaders responsible for modern media output, governance, adoption, and enterprise change.

## **Stakeholder Cards**

### **Chief Executive and Operating Leaders**

Leaders responsible for enterprise change, risk, efficiency, accountability, and operating discipline.

### **Chief Marketing and Brand Leaders**

Leaders responsible for campaign output, content volume, brand consistency, talent use, and public-facing media.

### **Chief Creative and Content Leaders**

Leaders responsible for production quality, creative control, new media models, and team adoption.

### **General Counsel and Rights Leaders**

Leaders responsible for rights, NILP®, consent, talent use, claims, reuse, legal escalation, and approval records.

### **Technology and Platform Leaders**

Leaders responsible for systems, agent access, metadata, integrations, records, and Creation Rights adoption.

### **Transformation and Operations Leaders**

Leaders responsible for moving the company from scattered practices to repeatable operating models.

### **Finance and Risk Leaders**

Leaders responsible for operational risk, evidence, auditability, insurance readiness, liability exposure, and cost control.

---

# **Section 5: The Executive View of Agents**

## **Eyebrow**

PRODUCTION AGENTS

## **Headline**

Agents need enterprise rules before they become enterprise practice.

## **Body**

Leaders do not need every team using private prompts, unmanaged tools, and unclear agent outputs.

They need a company model.

Production agents should have defined jobs, owners, approved tasks, blocked actions, operating files, access limits, review paths, and human checkpoints.

## **Executive Agent Questions**

Which agent types should the company allow?

Who owns each agent?

What files can agents access?

What outputs can agents prepare?

What actions are blocked?

Which outputs require human approval?

What review record should exist?

What risks need escalation?

What should connect to Creation Rights?

## **Core Line**

The agent does the prep.  
The human makes the call.  
The system records both.

---

# **Section 6: Human Approval and Accountability**

## **Eyebrow**

APPROVAL AND ACCOUNTABILITY

## **Headline**

Leaders need to know who can approve what.

## **Body**

As media production scales, informal approval becomes an enterprise risk.

Creation Skills helps leadership define the approval structure around media decisions.

## **Approval Areas**

Final creative  
Public posts  
Paid media  
Client-facing work  
Partner-facing work  
Talent-facing materials  
Product claims  
Creator deliverables  
Archive reuse  
Market versions  
Rights-sensitive work  
NILP® checks  
Consent review  
Final delivery  
Escalations

## **Leadership Outcome**

The company knows who reviews, who approves, what blocks movement, what escalates, and what record must exist before media moves forward.

---

# **Section 7: Rights, Talent, and NILP®**

## **Eyebrow**

RIGHTS AND NILP®

## **Headline**

Rights-sensitive media needs leadership-level structure.

## **Body**

Rights, talent, consent, NILP®, usage, territory, channel, format, and reuse questions now appear across many types of media.

They cannot be handled only at the end.

Creation Skills helps leaders place review earlier in the company process so teams understand when media can move, when it must stop, and what Creation Rights should govern.

## **Review Triggers**

Name used  
Image used  
Likeness used  
Persona used  
Voice used  
Creator content included  
Talent identifiable  
Third-party material included  
Paid media planned  
New territory added  
New channel added  
Usage window extended  
Asset reused  
Asset localized  
Asset cut down  
Contract limit may apply  
Public or partner delivery planned

## **Outcome**

Leadership gets a clearer model for controlling rights-sensitive media before risk concentrates at delivery.

---

# **Section 8: Records and Evidence**

## **Eyebrow**

RECORDS

## **Headline**

The company should know why media moved.

## **Body**

Executives need more than production output.

They need evidence that the right people approved the right media for the right use at the right time.

Creation Skills helps define the record model around media creation before Creation Rights carries the protected layer.

## **Record Areas**

Asset owner  
Approver

Rights status  
NILP® status  
Consent status

Usage scope  
Territory  
Channel  
Format  
Version  
Source material notes  
Claim review status  
Reuse status  
Escalation notes  
Final delivery status  
Audit reference

## **Outcome**

The company has a clearer path from media creation to protected records, audit, governance, and platform readiness.

---

# **Section 9: Business Outcomes**

## **Eyebrow**

BUSINESS OUTCOMES

## **Headline**

The goal is controlled scale.

## **Body**

Creation Skills helps leaders move from isolated experiments to an operating model the company can use across teams.

## **Outcomes**

### **Clearer Ownership**

Teams know who owns production paths, agents, operating files, approval gates, rights review, reuse, and records.

### **Safer Agent Use**

Production agents support the work without becoming uncontrolled decision makers.

### **Earlier Review**

Rights, NILP®, consent, talent use, product claims, and reuse questions appear before final delivery.

### **Stronger Records**

Approvals, rights notes, metadata, asset status, and delivery decisions are easier to capture.

### **Better Platform Adoption**

Creation Rights can govern a process the company has prepared, not one the platform has to repair.

### **Better Change Management**

Teams understand the new model, what changed, and how to operate it.

WHEN TO ACT

## **Headline**

The need usually appears before the platform rollout.

Creation Skills is often useful when leadership sees one or more signals that the current media process is not ready to scale.

WHY IT MATTERS

Governed media creation needs more than one kind of expertise.
