# **10\. Visual Modules**

## **Modules**

### **AgentNodeVisual**

Abstract node showing a production agent inside a controlled system.

### **ApprovalGateVisual**

Visual mark showing a human approval checkpoint.

### **AssetFlowVisual**

Routing line showing asset movement from brief to protected record.

### **RecordCardVisual**

Abstract metadata or protected record card.

### **OperatingFileVisual**

File-like module showing rules, examples, blocked actions, and owner.

### **RightsReviewVisual**

Checkpoint visual for rights, NILP®, consent, talent usage, and reuse.

### **EcosystemLayerVisual**

Three-layer visual:

Creation Skills  
CreativeIP.org  
Creation Rights

## **Visual Rule**

Visuals should support understanding.

Avoid decorative noise, fake unreadable text, robots, and generic AI glow.

---

# **11\. Content Data Structures**

## **Suggested Data Files**

### **navigation.ts**

Header links  
Dropdown links  
Footer columns  
CTA routes

### **engagements.ts**

Engagement cards and summaries.

### **audiences.ts**

Audience cards and summaries.

### **resources.ts**

Resource index and article metadata.

### **concepts.ts**

Concept page summaries and related links.

### **legal.ts**

Legal page metadata and review notices.

### **siteMeta.ts**

Default SEO title, description, social metadata, and brand settings.

### **forms.ts**

Form fields, select options, routing keys, and confirmation routes.

---

# **12\. Design Tokens**

## **Color Tokens**

### **Primary**

Lime green

### **Text**

Charcoal  
Near-black  
Muted gray

### **Backgrounds**

Warm white  
Soft neutral  
Near-black deep background  
Muted lime surface

### **Borders**

Soft gray  
Muted lime line  
Charcoal line

## **Type Tokens**

Large editorial headline  
Medium section headline  
Small uppercase eyebrow  
Readable body text  
Card title  
CTA label  
Legal small text

## **Spacing Tokens**

Hero spacing  
Section spacing  
Card gap  
Mobile section spacing  
Footer spacing

---

# **13\. Component QA**

## **Checklist**

Header appears consistently.  
Footer appears consistently.  
C/Skills. lockup is correct.  
Primary CTA style is consistent.  
Secondary CTA style is consistent.  
Hero components have correct hierarchy.  
Cards align across pages.  
Process steps are readable on mobile.  
Form labels are accessible.  
Confidentiality warning appears on all public forms.  
Legal-review notice appears where needed.  
No component uses lorem ipsum.  
No component uses fake unreadable design text.  
Mobile stacking works.  
Visual modules do not overpower copy.

---

# **14\. Build Rule**

## **Headline**

Use components to protect the brand system.

## **Body**

The Creation Skills site should feel like a coherent consulting brand with reusable patterns, not a collection of disconnected pages.

The component system should make it easy to publish future pages from the master document without redesigning the site each time.

## **Final Rule**

Every new page should be built from an existing template unless there is a strong reason to create a new pattern.

# Visual System and Art Direction

# **Creation Skills Internal Page: Visual System and Art Direction**

## **Page Identity**

**Creation Skills**  
**C/Skills.**  
**A Creation Rights Division**

## **Document Type**

Internal design direction document

## **Public Status**

Not a public page

## **Purpose**

This page defines the visual system for Creation Skills.

The goal is to make the brand feel creative, premium, editorial, and enterprise-ready without drifting into generic AI consulting, law firm design, software dashboard design, or public education branding.

Creation Skills should feel like a creative industry consulting practice with a strong lime lab identity.

---

# **1\. Visual Principle**

## **Headline**

Creative lab energy with enterprise control.

## **Body**

Creation Skills should look creative enough to belong in media and entertainment, but structured enough to be trusted by enterprise, legal, rights, platform, and executive teams.

The visual system should express both sides of the offer:

Creative production is moving faster.  
The company needs more control.

## **Visual Balance**

### **Creative**

Lime green energy  
Editorial layouts  
Bold typography  
Cut lines  
Graphic marks  
Production references  
Zine influence  
Media-lab feel

### **Controlled**

Structured sections  
Clear hierarchy  
Sharp cards  
Approval gates  
Linework  
Record motifs  
Metadata cues  
Human review points  
Platform readiness

## **Core Visual Idea**

Media moves through a controlled company flow:

Brief  
Production path  
Agent prep  
Operating file  
Human approval gate  
Rights review  
Protected record  
Creation Rights readiness

---

# **2\. Brand Feel**

## **Headline**

What the site should feel like.

## **Desired Feel**

Premium  
Creative  
Clear  
Confident  
Rights-aware  
Human-controlled  
Editorial  
Modern  
Slightly underground  
Enterprise polished  
Creative-industry native

## **Should Feel Like**

A high-end creative operations lab.  
A media systems advisory practice.  
A rights-aware production transformation studio.  
A serious consulting division inside the Creation Rights ecosystem.

## **Should Not Feel Like**

A generic SaaS dashboard.  
A law firm.  
A university program.  
A public course site.  
A generic AI consultancy.  
A corporate automation agency.  
A stock-photo business website.  
A Deloitte-style black and green advisory site.

---

# **3\. Color Direction**

## **Headline**

Lime green is the creative signal.

## **Primary Color**

### **Lime Green**

Use as the main identity color.

It should feel energetic, creative, sharp, and modern.

Use for:

CTA accents  
Graphic marks  
Section labels  
Linework  
Cards  
System diagrams  
Approval gate markers  
Agent nodes  
Hover states  
Editorial highlights

## **Supporting Colors**

### **Deep Charcoal**

Use for main text, deep backgrounds, footer, and high-contrast sections.

### **Warm White**

Use for primary page backgrounds.

The site should not feel cold or sterile.

### **Soft Neutral**

Use for cards, section backgrounds, utility surfaces, and form panels.

### **Muted Gray**

Use for secondary text, borders, metadata labels, and supporting UI details.

### **Lime Tint**

Use for subtle backgrounds, cards, and callout blocks.

## **Color Rules**

Lime should feel intentional, not decorative.

Do not use lime only as a button color.

Use lime as part of the full identity system: lines, labels, symbols, marks, and visual structure.

Keep contrast strong.

Do not place small lime text on white if it hurts readability.

---

# **4\. Typography Direction**

## **Headline**

Typography should feel editorial and precise.

## **Type Personality**

Confident  
Sharp  
Readable  
Modern  
Editorial  
Premium  
Structured

## **Headline Style**

Large, direct, and spacious.

Headlines should feel like strategic statements, not marketing fluff.

## **Body Style**

Readable, calm, and clear.

The content is complex, so typography must make it easier to understand.

## **Eyebrow Style**

Small uppercase labels.

Use for:

Page category  
Section type  
Engagement type  
Audience type  
Trust labels  
Internal planning labels

## **Typography Rules**

Avoid cramped text.

Avoid tiny gray body copy.

Avoid overly playful fonts.

Avoid generic startup typography.

Avoid making pages feel like a course module.

Use strong contrast between headline, subhead, and body.

---

# **5\. Layout Direction**

## **Headline**

The layout should feel spacious, modular, and editorial.

## **Layout Principles**

Large hero sections  
Generous negative space  
Sharp section breaks  
Clear reading flow  
Alternating content density  
Modular card systems  
Split panels  
Process rows  
Strong final CTAs  
Footer with clear ecosystem structure

## **Section Types**

### **Editorial Hero**

Large headline, strong subhead, focused body copy, two CTAs, and optional abstract system visual.

### **Split Panel**

Text on one side, visual or card stack on the other.

### **Card Grid**

Used for engagements, audiences, resources, topics, and deliverables.

### **Process Path**

Used for review, design, blueprint, pilot, implement, govern.

### **Principle Block**

Used for core lines:

The agent does the prep.  
The human makes the call.  
The system records both.

### **Ecosystem Split**

Used for Creation Skills, Creation Rights, and CreativeIP.org relationship.

### **Final CTA**

Strong closing section with one clear next action.

## **Layout Rules**

Do not make every section a card grid.

Do not make the site feel like a dashboard.

Do not overuse icons.

Do not crowd sections with too many small labels.

Let the page breathe.

---

# **6\. Graphic Motifs**

## **Headline**

The visual language should come from media movement and control.

## **Motifs to Use**

### **Agent Nodes**

Small connected marks representing production agents.

Should look abstract and controlled, not like robots.

### **Human Approval Gates**

Checkpoint marks, gates, stamps, or decision nodes.

Use to show where people approve before media moves.

### **Operating Files**

File cards, rule sheets, structured notes, prompt blocks, blocked-action lists, owner fields.

### **Rights Review Marks**

Small review labels around NILP®, consent, usage scope, talent, rights, reuse, and paid media.

### **Protected Records**

Metadata cards, record blocks, audit references, approval fields, asset status cards.

### **Asset Flow Lines**

Routing lines showing media moving from brief to delivery.

### **Cut Lines**

Editorial crop lines, production marks, frame edges, versioning marks.

### **Annotation Marks**

Small notes, arrows, labels, or brackets that give a zine/editorial feel.

## **Motifs to Avoid**

Robots  
Brain graphics  
Generic AI sparkles  
Cloud network clichés  
Stock office scenes  
Gavel or law icons  
Graduation caps  
Crypto-style nodes  
Overly literal padlocks  
Busy fake dashboards

---

# **7\. Imagery Direction**

## **Headline**

Use abstract production imagery, not stock people.

## **Preferred Image Style**

Abstract  
Editorial  
Graphic  
Lime-accented  
High contrast  
Layered  
System-based  
Media-production inspired  
No literal office photography

## **Image Concepts**

### **Controlled Media Path**

Abstract cards moving through a production path with agent nodes, approval gates, rights review, and protected records.

### **Operating File Stack**

Layered files with blocked actions, owner labels, examples, and review steps.

### **Human Approval Gate**

A bold gate mark in lime, sitting between agent prep and protected record.

### **Rights Review Map**

A visual field of usage scope, NILP®, consent, territory, channel, and reuse labels.

### **Protected Record Field**

Metadata cards arranged like editorial blocks with audit references and approval marks.

### **Creation Rights Readiness Layer**

A system diagram showing Creation Skills preparing the company before Creation Rights governs the protected record.

## **Image Rules**

No people unless specifically approved.

No fake unreadable text.

No random AI glow.

No generic 3D objects.

No sterile enterprise dashboard.

No law firm imagery.

No classroom imagery.

---

# **8\. Homepage Visual Direction**

## **Headline**

The homepage should immediately show the system.

## **Hero Visual Concept**

Show an abstract company flow:

Brief card  
Agent prep node  
Operating file  
Human approval gate  
Rights review checkpoint  
Protected record  
Creation Rights readiness marker

The visual should feel like an editorial production board, not a software interface.

## **Hero Layout**

Large headline left.  
Strong subhead.  
Two CTAs.  
Abstract system visual right or behind.  
Lime accents.  
Warm white or deep charcoal background.  
No busy hero image that competes with the copy.

## **Hero Copy Direction**

### **Headline**

Build the company flow behind modern media.

### **Subhead**

Creation Skills helps companies redesign how media moves through people, production agents, operating files, human approval gates, rights review, protected records, and Creation Rights readiness.

### **CTAs**

Book a C/Skills Review  
Explore Engagements

---

# **9\. Engagement Page Visual Direction**

## **Headline**

Engagement pages should feel productized and serious.

## **Visual Approach**

Each engagement should have a clear structure:

What it is  
When it fits  
What it covers  
What clients receive  
How it prepares Creation Rights readiness  
Final CTA

## **Engagement Card Style**

Sharp cards  
Simple labels  
Short descriptions  
Optional numeric marker  
Lime edge or corner mark  
No soft SaaS illustration cliché

## **Engagement Visual Motifs**

### **C/Skills Review**

Magnified production path.

### **Operating Model Design**

Future-state company flow.

### **Agent Operating Blueprint**

Agent profile and operating-file stack.

### **Governance Blueprint**

Approval matrix and escalation path.

### **Pilot Transformation**

Test path with repair notes.

### **Implementation Office**

Rollout path with owner guidance and updates.

---

# **10\. Resource Page Visual Direction**

## **Headline**

Resources should feel like field notes, not blog filler.

## **Visual Approach**

Resources should feel editorial, practical, and serious.

Use:

Article cards  
Topic labels  
Clean reading widths  
Strong headings  
Field-guide style layouts  
Lime annotation marks  
Minimal visual clutter

## **Resource Tone**

Short, useful, and connected to the main offer.

Every article should point back to:

Book a C/Skills Review  
Relevant engagement page  
Creation Rights readiness

## **Resource Avoid**

Generic thought leadership  
AI trend commentary  
Long academic article styling  
Course-like lesson modules  
Overdesigned blog cards

---

# **11\. Form Page Visual Direction**

## **Headline**

Forms should feel safe, serious, and easy to complete.

## **Form Design Rules**

Use clear labels.

Use enough spacing.

Use no file upload at launch.

Show confidentiality warning.

Make the primary CTA clear.

Keep the form visually calm.

Do not make the form look like a lead-gen trap.

## **Form Panel Style**

Warm white or soft neutral panel.  
Sharp border.  
Clear section labels.  
Strong submit button.  
Confidentiality warning in a visible but not alarming style.

## **Required Warning**

Do not send confidential source files, unreleased assets, private talent materials, contracts, legal documents, credentials, private links, or sensitive rights notes through this form.

---

# **12\. Footer Visual Direction**

## **Headline**

The footer should clarify the ecosystem.

## **Footer Needs**

Brand lockup  
Short description  
Clear navigation columns  
Ecosystem links  
Legal links  
CTA  
Copyright line  
NILP® mark language if approved

## **Footer Brand Block**

C/Skills.  
A Creation Rights Division

## **Footer Description**

Creation Skills helps companies prepare the company flow behind modern media creation: people, production agents, operating files, human approval gates, rights review, protected records, and Creation Rights readiness.

## **Ecosystem Line**

Creation Skills prepares the company.  
CreativeIP.org educates selected leaders.  
Creation Rights governs the protected layer.

---

# **13\. Mobile Direction**

## **Headline**

Mobile should feel just as premium as desktop.

## **Mobile Rules**

Keep navigation simple.

Keep CTA visible.

Stack cards cleanly.

Avoid tiny text.

Avoid too many side-by-side visual details.

Do not use busy backgrounds behind body copy.

Keep forms easy to complete.

Ensure process steps are readable.

Make final CTA strong.

## **Mobile Priority**

A visitor should understand in under one minute:

What Creation Skills does.  
Who it helps.  
Why Creation Rights readiness matters.  
How to book a C/Skills Review.

---

# **14\. Design Do and Do Not**

## **Do**

Use lime as an identity system.

Use strong typography.

Use negative space.

Use abstract media-system visuals.

Use clear card systems.

Use human approval gate motifs.

Use protected record motifs.

Use editorial structure.

Use premium restraint.

## **Do Not**

Use generic AI gradients.

Use robots.

Use stock corporate photography.

Use law firm imagery.

Use course or academy visuals.

Use unreadable fake text.

Use dashboard-heavy UI.

Use random icons everywhere.

Use cluttered zine style that hurts readability.

Use black and lime in a way that feels like Deloitte.

---

# **15\. Gemini Prompt Snippet**

## **Prompt**

Create a premium editorial visual system for Creation Skills, branded as C/Skills. A Creation Rights Division.

The visual system should feel like a lime green creative lab for governed media creation.

Represent media moving through a company flow:

Brief  
Production path  
Agent prep  
Operating file  
Human approval gate  
Rights review  
Protected record  
Creation Rights readiness

Use abstract production marks, file cards, routing lines, agent nodes, approval gates, metadata labels, and protected record symbols.

Make it creative, premium, structured, readable, and enterprise polished.

Avoid people, robots, generic AI gradients, stock photography, law firm imagery, course branding, fake unreadable text, and corporate dashboard clichés.

---

# **16\. Final Visual Rule**

## **Headline**

Make the system visible.

## **Body**

Creation Skills is not selling a vibe.

It is helping companies build the system behind modern media creation.

The visual design should make that system feel clear, creative, and controlled.

## **Core Visual Line**

Creative media moves fast.  
Creation Skills helps the company control how it moves.

# Build File Structure and Route Map

# **Creation Skills Internal Page: Build File Structure and Route Map**

## **Page Identity**

**Creation Skills**  
**C/Skills.**  
**A Creation Rights Division**

## **Document Type**

Internal build planning document

## **Public Status**

Not a public page

## **Purpose**

This page defines the recommended file structure, route structure, component organization, and content data approach for building the Creation Skills website.

The goal is to keep the build clean, reusable, and easy to expand as phase two, phase three, resource, and ecosystem pages are added later.

The site should be static-first, fast, accessible, and easy to maintain.

---

# **1\. Build Principle**

## **Headline**

Build the launch site as a reusable content system.

## **Body**

Creation Skills has a large content library.

The first public site should launch with a focused set of pages, but the codebase should be ready to support future concept pages, audience pages, resources, ecosystem pages, and utility pages without rebuilding the design from scratch.

## **Core Rule**

Build once.  
Reuse templates.  
Add pages from structured content.

---

# **2\. Recommended Route Groups**

## **Core Routes**

/  
/what-we-do  
/engagements  
/who-we-help  
/creation-rights-readiness  
/about  
/book-review  
/contact

## **Engagement Routes**

/engagements/cskills-review  
/engagements/operating-model-design  
/engagements/agent-operating-blueprint  
/engagements/governance-blueprint  
/engagements/pilot-transformation  
/engagements/implementation-office

## **Utility Routes**

/privacy  
/terms  
/cookies  
/accessibility  
/legal-notice  
/404

## **Thank-You Routes**

/thank-you/review  
/thank-you/contact  
/thank-you/partnership  
/thank-you/briefing  
/thank-you/newsletter

## **Optional Resource Routes**

/resources  
/resources/why-production-agents-need-owners  
/resources/from-loose-prompts-to-operating-files  
/resources/the-human-approval-gate  
/resources/why-rights-review-belongs-earlier  
/resources/creation-rights-readiness

## **Phase Two Concept Routes**

/concepts/company-owned-production-agents  
/concepts/operating-files  
/concepts/human-approval-gates  
/concepts/rights-review-and-nilp-checks

/concepts/asset-reuse-and-usage-controls  
/concepts/decision-rights-and-escalation-paths  
/concepts/responsible-agent-use  
/concepts/confidentiality-and-client-materials  
/concepts/deliverables-and-operating-assets  
/concepts/how-we-work  
/concepts/terms-and-language

## **Phase Three Audience Routes**

/who-we-help/media-and-entertainment-enterprises  
/who-we-help/brands-with-large-content-operations  
/who-we-help/agencies-and-production-companies

/who-we-help/legal-rights-and-risk-teams  
/who-we-help/technology-platform-and-digital-asset-teams  
/who-we-help/executive-leaders-and-transformation-sponsors

Additional audience routes can be added later as phase three expands.

---

# **3\. Suggested Next.js App Structure**

## **Recommended App Directory**

app/  
app/page.tsx  
app/layout.tsx  
app/not-found.tsx

app/what-we-do/page.tsx  
app/engagements/page.tsx  
app/engagements/\[slug\]/page.tsx  
app/who-we-help/page.tsx  
app/who-we-help/\[slug\]/page.tsx  
app/creation-rights-readiness/page.tsx  
app/about/page.tsx  
app/book-review/page.tsx  
app/contact/page.tsx

app/resources/page.tsx  
app/resources/\[slug\]/page.tsx

app/concepts/\[slug\]/page.tsx

app/privacy/page.tsx  
app/terms/page.tsx  
app/cookies/page.tsx  
app/accessibility/page.tsx  
app/legal-notice/page.tsx

app/thank-you/review/page.tsx  
app/thank-you/contact/page.tsx  
app/thank-you/partnership/page.tsx  
app/thank-you/briefing/page.tsx  
app/thank-you/newsletter/page.tsx

## **Notes**

Use dynamic routes for engagement pages, resource pages, concept pages, and audience pages.

This keeps the build easier to expand later.

Static generation should be preferred wherever possible.

---

components/

# **5\. Page Content Model**

## **Headline**

Each page should be built from structured content.

## **Recommended Page Object**

type PageContent \= {  
  slug: string  
  title: string  
  seoTitle: string  
  seoDescription: string  
  pageType: string  
  launchStatus: string  
  hero: {  
    eyebrow: string  
    headline: string  
    subhead?: string  
    body?: string  
    primaryCta?: CTA  
    secondaryCta?: CTA  
  }  
  sections: PageSection\[\]  
  finalCta?: {  
    headline: string  
    body: string  
    primaryCta: CTA  
    secondaryCta?: CTA  
  }  
}

## **CTA Object**

type CTA \= {  
  label: string  
  href: string  
}

## **Section Object**

type PageSection \= {  
  type: "text" | "cards" | "process" | "split" | "principle" | "form" | "notice"  
  eyebrow?: string  
  headline?: string  
  body?: string  
  cards?: Card\[\]  
  steps?: Step\[\]  
}

---

# **6\. Engagement Data Model**

## **Purpose**

Engagement pages should use one shared template.

## **Engagement Fields**

slug  
title  
seoTitle  
seoDescription  
eyebrow  
headline  
subhead  
body  
whenItFits  
whatItCovers  
whatClientsReceive  
creationRightsReadiness  
whatGoodLooksLike  
primaryCta  
secondaryCta

## **Engagement Slugs**

cskills-review  
operating-model-design  
agent-operating-blueprint  
governance-blueprint  
pilot-transformation  
implementation-office

## **Rule**

Do not hardcode each engagement page as a separate visual design unless needed.

Use one engagement template with different content.

---

# **7\. Audience Data Model**

## **Purpose**

Audience pages should be easy to add later.

## **Audience Fields**

slug  
title  
seoTitle  
seoDescription  
eyebrow  
headline  
subhead  
body  
audienceProblem  
whatWeHelpWith  
useCases  
productionAgents  
operatingFiles  
approvalGates  
creationRightsReadiness  
engagementPaths  
finalCta

## **Launch Audience Page**

who-we-help should use audience cards instead of full audience detail pages at first.

## **Phase Three Rule**

Audience detail pages should remain unpublished until reviewed and internally linked.

---

# **8\. Resource Data Model**

## **Purpose**

Resources should feel like field notes, not generic blog posts.

## **Resource Fields**

slug  
title  
seoTitle  
seoDescription  
category  
eyebrow  
headline  
subhead  
body  
sections  
relatedEngagement  
relatedConcept  
primaryCta  
secondaryCta

## **Resource Categories**
